name: Bear River Range
short_description: BLM rockhounding in the Bear River Range foothills near Logan. Find quartz, agate, and jasper in steep alpine terrain at 6200+ feet elevation.
description:
The Bear River Range offers public BLM rockhounding opportunities in the Cache County foothills east of Logan, Utah. This area sits within the Wasatch Range province where Precambrian metamorphic rocks and Paleozoic quartzites were uplifted during the Laramide Orogeny, creating excellent conditions for quartz and chalcedony mineralization. The steep terrain at 6283 feet elevation means accessing collecting areas requires moderate hiking, but the alpine setting and abundant micro-crystalline quartz deposits make the effort worthwhile.

Rockhounders visiting the Bear River Range typically search washes, alluvial fans, and exposed ridge outcrops where weathering has exposed quartz-rich material. The area produces quality agate nodules and jasper specimens, particularly in areas where volcanic activity during the Tertiary period deposited silica-rich fluids into fractures and vugs. Early morning collecting is essential due to steep slopes and afternoon thunderstorm potential.

This location is best suited for rockhounders with moderate experience and good hiking fitness. The BLM land here allows recreational collecting under personal use rules - no permit required, no commercial activity, and a 25 pound daily limit per FLPMA regulations. The steep terrain and high elevation mean this is not an ideal beginner or family site, but experienced collectors will find productive collecting grounds and solitude.

Access to productive areas typically requires 1-3 miles of hiking through sagebrush and scattered aspen. Cell service is spotty throughout the range. Always bring plenty of water, sturdy boots, and a headlamp - afternoon thunderstorms develop quickly in this terrain, and steep slopes make descent hazardous in low light.
history: The Bear River Range sits at the junction of the Wasatch Range and the Basin and Range province. Precambrian metamorphic basement rocks and Paleozoic quartzite sequences were steeply tilted and uplifted during Laramide compression 75-55 million years ago, creating the ridge-and-valley topography visible today. Tertiary silica-rich hydrothermal fluids migrated through fractures in these uplifted sequences, depositing the agate, chalcedony, and quartz specimens found by modern rockhounders.
tips: Search alluvial fans and washes at the base of steeper ridges where weathered quartz material accumulates - the best specimens typically erode out of outcrop during spring runoff. Bring a 10x loupe to examine agate banding and chalcedony color quality before collecting - much of the surface material shows weathering. Look for the pink to white quartz nodules in weathered Paleozoic quartzite outcrops on south-facing slopes where erosion is most active. Early morning collecting is critical - afternoon thunderstorms develop rapidly above 6000 feet, and steep terrain becomes dangerous in rain or poor visibility.
terrain_notes: Steep alpine terrain at 6283 feet elevation with sagebrush, scattered aspen, and exposed quartzite outcrops. Slope is consistently steep with loose talus and scree in many areas. Parking near BLM access points typically involves unimproved pullouts. Expect 1-3 miles of hiking on no-trail terrain to reach productive collecting areas. Footing is rocky and requires sturdy boots. The terrain is not wheelchair accessible and requires good hiking fitness.
written_directions: From Logan, take US 89/91 east toward Bear Lake for approximately 8 miles. Turn right (south) on Idaho State Road 89 toward Bloomington. Continue 6 miles and look for BLM land access points marked by small signs on the west side of the road near the Bear River drainage. Park at unimproved pullouts and hike upslope into wash systems and exposed ridge areas. GPS coordinates 41.63284, -111.667856 mark a productive area accessible via steep hiking from the foothills.
road_conditions: US 89/91 is paved. Access roads to parking areas are unimproved dirt and may require high-clearance vehicle. Passenger vehicles can access some pullouts, but 4x4/AWD recommended for deeper access.
parking_notes: Unimproved pullouts along BLM land boundaries near the Bear River drainage. No developed parking areas. Capacity varies from 2-5 vehicles per pullout. No facilities.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. All collecting limited to surface material and loose specimens - no hammering into solid bedrock. Respect private property boundaries.
rules:

Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA

No motorized excavation

nearest_services: Logan, Utah - 12 miles west. Gas, food, water, lodging available.
accessibility_notes: Steep alpine terrain with loose talus, scree, and no maintained trails. Requires 1-3 miles of hiking over rough terrain with significant elevation change. Not suitable for limited mobility or families with young children. Sturdy boots and good fitness required. Afternoon thunderstorms create flash flood risk in washes. Not wheelchair accessible.

faq.question: What gemstones can you find rockhounding near Logan in the Bear River Range?
faq.answer: The Bear River Range near Logan produces quality quartz, agate, jasper, and chalcedony specimens. Agate nodules with distinctive banding are common in weathered Paleozoic quartzite outcrops. Micro-crystalline quartz and chalcedony are found in alluvial fans and washes, especially at the base of steep ridges where weathering concentrates the material.

faq.question: Do you need a permit to rockhound on BLM land in Cache County, Utah?
faq.answer: No permit is required for recreational rockhounding on BLM land in Cache County, including the Bear River Range area. Personal use collecting is allowed under FLPMA regulations with a 25 pound daily limit. No fees apply, but commercial collection is not permitted.

faq.question: What is the best season for rockhounding the Bear River Range foothills?
faq.answer: June through September is the optimal season. The high elevation (6283 feet) means snow blocks access before June. Afternoon thunderstorms are common July-August but typically clear by evening. After September, early season snow makes the steep terrain hazardous.

faq.question: How difficult is hiking to find agate in the Bear River Range near Logan?
faq.answer: The terrain is moderate to difficult. Expect 1-3 miles of steep hiking on no-trail terrain with loose talus and scree. The high elevation and steep slopes make this suitable for experienced rockhounders with good fitness. Families with young children should consider easier sites. Sturdy boots and early morning starts are essential.

faq.question: What tools and supplies do you need for rockhounding the Bear River Range?
faq.answer: Bring sturdy hiking boots, a 10x loupe for examining agate quality, a hand pick or chisel for prying loose specimens, a backpack, and plenty of water (1-2 gallons for a day hike). A headlamp is essential - afternoon thunderstorms develop rapidly and visibility drops quickly. Sunscreen and a hat are critical at 6200+ feet elevation.

faq.question: Is the Bear River Range a good rockhounding spot for beginners, or is it better for experienced collectors?
faq.answer: The Bear River Range is best suited for moderate to experienced rockhounders. The steep terrain, high elevation, afternoon thunderstorm risk, and remote access make it challenging for families and beginners. However, the abundant quartz and agate specimens and the solitude attract serious collectors willing to hike difficult terrain.
